explicit_text: My full name is William Stanley Atherton, born on April 12, 1933, in Ponca City, Oklahoma. I grew up surrounded by entrepreneurial influences, especially from my father and grandfather. Over the years, I've witnessed and been part of many family ventures and developments, and even at 88 years old, I remain active in business deals in Tulsa.

explicit_text: My father, Jess William Atherton, was born in 1890 in Caney, Kansas. He was a remarkable man—an engineer and entrepreneur who ran the Atherton Grading Company and other businesses like a laundry service and an airline called Bluebird Airways. He was a pioneer pilot and a hard worker who built Indian Hills Lake and developed real estate around it. I learned a lot about business and perseverance from him.

explicit_text: My father owned the Atherton Grading Company, which specialized in dirt moving and construction work, especially for the oil industry. He managed teams of horses and mules to build roads and locations for oil rigs, and later cleaned up those sites after drilling was done. It was one of his main enterprises and showed his ability to adapt and find business opportunities.

explicit_text: Indian Hills Lake was a unique development my father created about five miles east of Ponca City on the way to Pawhuska. He built the dam himself with horses and mules, creating a beautiful lake and selling lots for cabins around it. We lived in a house he built near a smaller lake below the main one, and the lake still exists today.

explicit_text: My grandfather participated in the Oklahoma Land Run of 1893, the largest land run in U.S. history, which opened the Cherokee Strip for settlement. They came from Caney, Kansas, and settled near Stillwater in Glencoe, Oklahoma. I heard stories about how they raced to claim land by finding survey posts and registering their claims in Perry, Oklahoma.

explicit_text: I vividly remember the Dust Bowl years from 1930 to 1936. The sky would get so dark with dust you couldn’t see the sun, and my mother would wet towels to keep the dust from coming in through the windows and doors. It was hard to breathe outside, and the dust even left a heavy smell inside our home.
